text: The surgeon works laterally, sweeping tissues down and away to connect anatomical planes. The posterior rectus sheath is visible, extending downwards as the surgeon navigates the frozen suprapubic space due to previous prostatectomy.

The surgeon carefully isolates the hernia sac, sweeping tissues laterally while navigating the sticky adhesions. The appendix remains visibly trapped within the hernia. The surgeon pulls back towards the trocar to detach the bladder from the pubic bone, approaching the iliopubic tract.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the sac, opening it to reveal the appendix lurking below. The appendix is visible as the surgeon pulls up and twists the sac to encourage the appendix to fall away from its position.

The surgeon sweeps the chord structures down from the hernia sac, revealing the appendix being pulled upward. The appendix appears fused to the hernia sac, prompting the surgeon to carefully pull laterally to avoid disrupting its blood supply, ensuring safety by maintaining a high and lateral approach.

The surgeon carefully isolates the spermatic cord, working medially with gentle sweeps and cautery application. The areolar tissue becomes visible as it is teased away from the surrounding structures.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the flap to ensure adequate space for the mesh placement, preserving the blood supply. The mesh is laid out, and preparations are made for suturing it in a standard fashion.

The surgeon finishes stitching the mesh, running it medially, and addresses the appendix and lower flap. The top flap is completed first, followed by reconstruction of the lower flap, splitting it down the middle of the mesh to the epigastrics. The V-lock suture is used from top down, cinching it up to expose and close the opening, anchoring it superiorly to hold the structure in place.

The surgeon focuses on the appendix while cinching thickened flaps and hernia sacs. Bipolar cautery is applied to vessels within the flap and hernia sac. The mesoappendix boundary is unclear, prompting careful skiving of the flap and peritoneal closure. A visible hole is noted, large enough for an interparietal hernia.
